Simplify this expression: i - prefixOnes[i] + totalOnes - prefixOnes[i] = i + totalOnes - 2*prefixOnes[i].

List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();

int totalOnes = 0;

for (int num : nums) totalOnes += num;

int currentOnes = 0;

int maxScore = Integer.MIN_VALUE;

for (int i=0; i <= nums.length; i++) {

    int score = i + totalOnes - 2*currentOnes;

    if (score > maxScore) {

        maxScore = score;

        result.clear();

        result.add(i);

    } else if (score == maxScore) {

        result.add(i);

    }

    // Now, update currentOnes if i < nums.length

    if (i < nums.length) {

        currentOnes += nums[i];

    }

}

return result;

Now, implement this in Java.

Now, code:

public List<Integer> maxScoreIndices(int[] nums) {

        List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
        int totalOnes = 0;
        for (int num : nums) {
            totalOnes += num;
        }
        int currentOnes = 0;
        int maxScore = Integer.MIN_VALUE;
        for (int i = 0; i <= nums.length; i++) {
            int score = i + totalOnes - 2 * currentOnes;
            if (score > maxScore) {
                maxScore = score;
                result.clear();
                result.add(i);
            } else if (score == maxScore) {
                result.add(i);
            }
            if (i < nums.length) {
                currentOnes += nums[i];
            }
        }
        return result;
    }

Yes. This should work.

Now, check for efficiency.

Time complexity: O(n), since we have one pass to compute totalOnes, then another pass of O(n) steps. All operations inside are O(1).

Space complexity: O(1) except for the output list. The output can be up to O(n) in the worst case (if all splits have same score), but the problem says to return it in any order. The starter code uses List<Integer>, which is acceptable.

Efficiency is HIGH.

Energy implications: LOW, since it's a linear scan with minimal operations.

Explanation: The approach calculates the score for each split using a derived formula that allows O(1) per split after precomputing totalOnes. It uses a single pass to track currentOnes and compute scores dynamically, ensuring optimal time and space efficiency.

solution: ```java
class Solution {
    public List<Integer> maxScoreIndices(int[] nums) {
        List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
        int totalOnes = 0;
        for (int num : nums) {
            totalOnes += num;
        }
        int currentOnes = 0;
        int maxScore = Integer.MIN_VALUE;
        for (int i = 0; i <= nums.length; i++) {
            int score = i + totalOnes - 2 * currentOnes;
            if (score > maxScore) {
                maxScore = score;
                result.clear();
                result.add(i);
            } else if (score == maxScore) {
                result.add(i);
            }
            if (i < nums.length) {
                currentOnes += nums[i];
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1) auxiliary (excluding output)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution dynamically computes the division score for each split using a derived formula that leverages prefix sums implicitly. It tracks the number of 1s encountered so far and uses the total number of 1s in the array to calculate scores in O(1) per split. This avoids explicit prefix arrays, achieving optimal linear time and constant space (excluding output). The approach efficiently identifies maximum scores and collects all indices achieving it.
